title: DSAR request-type mix anomaly — zero-deletion skew indicating broken workflow
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate the correctness of an individual DSAR outcome; only the statistical mix across the programme.
evidence: Saved search 'dsar_request_type_mix' (weekly) compares the current-week request-type distribution against a rolling 12-week baseline and alerts on >3σ drift.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A month with zero erasures across a 10,000-subject controller is a statistical anomaly worth investigating; this UC raises the signal.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of GDPR Art.12(2) — Splunk UC-22.36.5: DSAR request-type mix anomaly — zero-deletion skew indicating broken workflow.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.5' running on DSAR ticketing system request-type field, historic aggregates from compliance summary index., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CCPA reporting metrics require accurate categorisation; a sudden request-type skew often means a broken form.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of CCPA §1798.130(a) — Splunk UC-22.36.5: DSAR request-type mix anomaly — zero-deletion skew indicating broken workflow.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.36.5' running on DSAR ticketing system request-type field, historic aggregates from compliance summary index., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A code change accidentally routes all 'delete my data' buttons to the access-request endpoint; in the following week the UC fires with a 99% access / 1% deletion mix (baseline 60/40).
controlTest.negativeScenario: Normal week-over-week fluctuation in request-type mix does NOT fire.

dataSources: DSAR ticketing system request-type field, historic aggregates from compliance summary index.
spl:
index=dsar sourcetype=onetrust:dsar OR sourcetype=servicenow:privacy earliest=-84d
| bin _time span=7d
| stats count by _time request_type
| eventstats sum(count) as total by _time
| eval ratio=count/total
| stats avg(ratio) as baseline, stdev(ratio) as baseline_sd latest(ratio) as current by request_type
| eval drift=abs(current-baseline)
| where drift>(3*baseline_sd) AND baseline_sd>0
| table request_type baseline current drift baseline_sd
description: Detects statistically anomalous drift in the distribution of DSAR request types (access / rectification / erasure / portability / opt-out). A sudden zero-erasure week across a large estate is usually a symptom of a broken UX flow rather than genuine user choice.
value: Makes the common 'silent bug in the privacy portal' failure mode visible without waiting for a complaint.
implementation: (1) Ensure DSAR events emit a canonical request_type from an allow-list; (2) run weekly; (3) on fire, open a DPO + engineering review ticket. Indexes required: `index=dsar`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=onetrust:dsar`, `sourcetype=servicenow:privacy`.
visualization: Stacked area of request-type mix over 12 weeks, bar chart of current-vs-baseline deltas, single-value 'drift categories this week'.
knownFalsePositives: Seasonal campaigns (e.g., year-end 'delete my data' reminders) legitimately shift the mix; maintain a known-campaign lookup to dampen the signal for those windows.
grandmaExplanation: We check on detects statistically anomalous drift in the distribution of dsar request types (access / rectification / erasure / portability / opt-out). a sudden zero-erasure week across a large estate is usual. This helps maintain steady compliance posture and reduces manual review effort.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: DSAR ticketing system request-type field, historic aggregates from compliance summary index..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ensure DSAR events emit a canonical request_type from an allow-list; (2) run weekly; (3) on fire, open a DPO + engineering review ticket.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=dsar sourcetype=onetrust:dsar OR sourcetype=servicenow:privacy earliest=-84d
| bin _time span=7d
| stats count by _time request_type
| eventstats sum(count) as total by _time
| eval ratio=count/total
| stats avg(ratio) as baseline, stdev(ratio) as baseline_sd latest(ratio) as current by request_type
| eval drift=abs(current-baseline)
| where drift>(3*baseline_sd) AND baseline_sd>0
| table request_type baseline current drift baseline_sd
```

Understanding this SPL

**DSAR request-type mix anomaly — zero-deletion skew indicating broken workflow** — Makes the common 'silent bug in the privacy portal' failure mode visible without waiting for a complaint.

Documented **Data sources**: DSAR ticketing system request-type field, historic aggregates from compliance summary index. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: dsar; **sourcetype**: onetrust:dsar, servicenow:privacy. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=dsar, sourcetype=onetrust:dsar, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` aggregates events by **_time request_type**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **ratio** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `stats` aggregates events by **request_type**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **drift** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where drift>(3*baseline_sd) AND baseline_sd>0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: _time, baseline, baseline_sd, current, drift, ratio, request_type.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Stacked area of request-type mix over 12 weeks, bar chart of current-vs-baseline deltas, single-value 'drift categories this week'.
